title: Ivory Field Allover Vase and Palmette Persian Carpet
lead: A large square-format carpet woven on an ivory ground with an uninterrupted allover lattice of curving vine, palmette, and rosette — the drawing continuous yet never crowded, its warm terracotta and sage tones held in careful balance.

designFeatures: Reciprocal ivory main border with scrolling floral meander
Narrow sage guard stripes framing the field
distinguishing: Near-square proportions are atypical and suggest a special commission or palace-scale order.
The palette shows deliberate oxidation softening — consistent with natural dye wool of the late Qajar period.
Field drawing maintains precise vertical and diagonal symmetry across a very large area, indicating a master cartoon.

title: Antique Persian Tabriz All-Over Floral Gold Field
lead: Woven on a warm gold field, this Tabriz carries a continuous vine-and-blossom lattice across its full expanse, the repeat tightening at the border into a scrolling meander punctuated by palmettes and paired lancet leaves in soft teal.

designFeatures: Symmetrical rosette clusters at field intervals
distinguishing: Unusually warm saffron-gold ground, rare for Tabriz production of this period.
Teal leaf accents in border corners suggest influence of late Qajar court atelier conventions.
Field pattern retains legibility across the full length, indicating even wear and quality of original dyestuffs.

title: Ivory-Ground Sarouk Farahan Floral Medallion Carpet
lead: An ivory-ground Sarouk Farahan of the late Qajar period, its field organized around a diamond-shaped reserve densely populated with herati-influenced palmettes, lancet leaves, and reciprocating vine scrolls rendered in terracotta, cobalt, and sage.

designFeatures: Diamond-shaped central reserve on open ivory field
Terracotta main border with stylized floral cartouches
Navy inner guard stripe with diagonal stripe accent panel
Symmetrical bilateral composition with quarter-repeat corners
distinguishing: The diagonal-stripe spandrel fill in the upper field is an unusual regional detail, suggesting a transitional workshop between Farahan and Sarouk production.
Ivory grounds of this density are less common in Sarouk Farahan work and typically command heightened collector interest.
Natural dye palette shows characteristic abrash variation in the terracotta border field.

title: Ivory Field Bakhtiari Tree-of-Life Floral Rug
lead: A west-Persian Bakhtiari on an open ivory ground, its central axis flowering outward in the characteristic tree-of-life arrangement — birds, rosettes, and palmettes distributed with the deliberate informality that distinguishes tribal work from court production.

distinguishing: Ivory ground is less common in Bakhtiari work, which more typically employs red or blue fields, adding brightness and legibility to the dense patterning.
Paired bird figures flanking the central stem reflect an animist decorative vocabulary persistent in tribal Bakhtiari weaving through the mid-twentieth century.
The border's running geometric reciprocal pattern contrasts effectively with the naturalistic field, a hallmark of the Chahar Mahal workshops.

title: Antique Persian Heriz Large Room Carpet
lead: A well-traveled Heriz of the early twentieth century, carrying the characteristic geometric medallion and angular vine lattice that made these village looms the workhorses of the Northwest Persian tradition. Warm camel and brick tones show honest age.

designFeatures: Wide ivory border with scrolling reciprocal vine
distinguishing: Unusual warmth in the camel-gold ground, suggesting natural undyed wool rather than synthetic dye
Border proportion is generous relative to field, characteristic of earlier Heriz production
Minor color oxidation in deep brown areas consistent with natural iron-based dyes

title: Arts and Crafts Style Palmette Lattice Carpet
lead: A repeating fan-palmette lattice in slate blue, warm brown, and ivory animates the field in staggered rows. The border carries stylized foliate scrollwork reminiscent of William Morris textile designs, suggesting an Anglo-Indian commission of the late colonial period.

designFeatures: Staggered fan-palmette repeat across open field
Foliate scroll border with Arts and Crafts character
Multi-guard stripe framing in dark brown and gold
Stylized bird and acanthus motifs in outer border
Tonal interplay between blue ground and brown drawing
distinguishing: Design vocabulary closely aligned with late 19th-century English reform taste, likely produced for export to Britain or the United States.
The outer border's bird-and-branch frieze recalls Iznik-influenced pattern books adapted by Indian workshops for Western clientele.
Consistent drawing quality and even color distribution suggest a well-organized commercial atelier rather than village production.

title: Antique Kazak Triple-Medallion Field Rug
lead: A classic Southwest Caucasian composition presenting three bold stepped medallions on a saturated rust-red ground, each medallion rendered in indigo, ivory, and gold, framed by a lively reciprocal-border system characteristic of the Kazak tradition.

designFeatures: Hooked geometric filler motifs on open field
Crab-pattern secondary border in ivory and red
distinguishing: Central ivory-ground medallion offers tonal contrast to flanking indigo examples, a hallmark of mature Kazak composition.
Corner brackets with large triangle motifs in the outer border suggest attribution to the Borjalu or Lori-Pambak sub-district.
Natural dye palette retains exceptional saturation consistent with late 19th-century pre-synthetic production.

title: Caucasian Kazak Three-Medallion Field Carpet
lead: A bold terracotta field carries three stacked medallions — ivory, indigo, and ivory — each worked in the angular tribal geometry that defines the Kazak tradition. Flanking motifs include stylized eagles, hourglass forms, and serrated leaf sprays.

designFeatures: Serrated diamond medallions in alternating ivory and indigo
Stylized eagle and bird-of-prey field motifs
Multi-guard border with octagonal cartouche outer band
Angular leaf-spray and rosette fill ornaments
distinguishing: The alternating ivory-indigo-ivory medallion sequence is characteristic of the Lori-Pambak sub-group within the broader Kazak weaving tradition.
Outer border octagonal medallions echo Lesghi star compositions, suggesting cross-regional design exchange.
Saturated natural dyes show even tone throughout the field with no visible oxidation or fading.

title: Caucasian Ivory Field Allover Boteh Lattice Rug
lead: An ivory-ground Caucasian scatter worked on a fine diagonal lattice, each lozenge cradling a stylized boteh or rosette in saffron, coral, navy, and brown — the overall effect dense yet orderly, characteristic of late nineteenth-century Shirvan weaving.

distinguishing: Ivory ground is relatively uncommon in Shirvan production and commands particular collector interest.
Natural dye variation across the field suggests the rug was woven in sections over time, adding character to the surface.
Small white label visible at lower center edge — easily removed without affecting pile.

title: Caucasian Kazak Four-Panel Medallion Rug
lead: A classic Kazak square-format piece organized around four bold cartouche panels, each carrying a variant of the crab or cloudband medallion on alternating navy and madder grounds. The ivory lattice dividers and staggered palette give the field a vivid, architectonic rhythm.

designFeatures: Diagonal barber-pole stripe outer guard borders
Alternating madder and navy ground within panels
distinguishing: The square format is characteristic of Kazak bagfaces or small scatter rugs produced for local domestic use rather than export.
Color alternation across the four cartouches — no two adjacent panels share the same ground — is a deliberate compositional strategy common to late 19th-century Borchalu and Lori-Pambak weavings.
Natural dye integrity appears strong; the madder retains warmth without the brown-fade associated with later synthetic overdyeing.

title: Antique Caucasian Shirvan All-Over Geometric Field Rug
lead: A late 19th-century Shirvan woven in the village tradition of the eastern Caucasus, its field filled with repeating hooked geometric motifs across a terracotta and dark-chocolate ground, framed by multiple reciprocal star-and-arrow guard borders.

designFeatures: All-over repeating hooked geometric lattice field
Multiple reciprocal guard borders with eight-pointed stars
Diamond medallion accents within field grid
Greek-key meander elements at field transitions
Chevron and arrow-head outer border framing
distinguishing: Oxidized dark brown wool in field areas indicates authentic natural dye and age-consistent wool corrosion typical of genuine late 19th-century Shirvan production.
The density and regularity of the border system — seven distinct bands — points to a weaver of considerable experience working within an established workshop or village tradition.
Abrash visible in mid-field terracotta tone, consistent with hand-spun wool dyed in separate lots across an extended weaving period.

title: Caucasian Kazak Three-Medallion Dark Navy Field
lead: A bold three-medallion Kazak field in deep indigo carries interlocking geometric forms in aged gold, terracotta, and soft slate, flanked by serrated palmette borders that frame the composition with characteristic regional authority.

designFeatures: Three stacked geometric medallions on open field
Serrated palmette and rosette border system
Reciprocal running vine inner guard border
Scattered small animal and botanical field fillers
distinguishing: Woven inscription or date cartouche visible in lower right field — consistent with weaver's mark tradition.
Ivory field central medallion provides deliberate tonal contrast between the three primary forms.
Border ivory ground with dense polychrome patterning reflects classic South Caucasian workshop practice.

title: Turkish Ivory Field Floral Botanical Runner
lead: A measured ivory field carries repeating botanical columns — stylized tulip blossoms and feathered palmette sprays rendered in soft taupe, coral, and brick — framed by a scrolling vine border on a warm gold ground.

designFeatures: Repeating tulip and palmette botanical columns
Feathered leaf sprays flanking central stems
Scrolling vine-and-blossom border on gold ground
Corner rosette accents in brick red
Open ivory field with minimal ground interruption
distinguishing: Tulip motif placement echoes 16th-century Ottoman court textile tradition adapted for village production
Three-column lattice arrangement creates strong vertical rhythm suited to hallway or runner installation
Restrained coral-and-taupe palette gives the piece unusual quietness for Anatolian work of this period

title: Faded Anatolian Yastik-Scale Medallion Scatter Rug
lead: A heavily abraded Central Anatolian scatter piece whose camel-and-sage ground has softened over a century of use, leaving the stacked medallion field and stepped border legible but quiet—a rug that wears its age honestly.

distinguishing: Pronounced overall abrash from natural wool dye variation across warp rows
Burgundy selvedge overcast intact on both long sides
Lower field panel appears intentionally open—may reflect a prayer-format influence

title: Faded Anatolian Medallion Rug with Inscription Panel
lead: A well-worn Central Anatolian example carrying a single lozenge medallion centered on a field of softened camel and silver-grey. A cartouche with woven inscription near the upper medallion edge points to a dated or dedicatory commission.

distinguishing: Inscription panel near upper medallion likely contains a date or maker's name in Arabic script.
Pronounced natural abrash across the field suggests organically dyed wool spun in separate batches.
Overall patina and pile reduction consistent with genuine age and domestic use over many decades.

title: Antique Turkish Oushak in Sage and Coral
lead: A large-format Oushak woven in the classic palette of soft sage green and faded coral, its open field punctuated by scattered floral and geometric medallion forms characteristic of the western Anatolian tradition at the turn of the century.

designFeatures: Coral and ivory border with repeating vine scroll
distinguishing: Natural wool dye oxidation lends depth to the green ground.
Wide ivory inner border with coral floral meander frames the field with measured formality.
Scale appropriate for a principal reception room or dining hall.

title: Neoclassical Allover Medallion Circle Pattern European Carpet
lead: A measured field of concentric medallion circles, each centering a wreath motif, repeats across a pale celadon ground. The palette — bone, sage, and soft blue — recalls Aubusson workshop sensibility translated into pile.

designFeatures: Tonal gradient shading within each medallion
Plain narrow border in celadon blue
distinguishing: Faint rosette accents in terracotta visible along upper register, suggesting original design included polychrome highlights now softened with age.
The tight repeat and classical iconography align with early-to-mid 20th-century European workshop production in the Savonnerie or Axminster tradition.
Exceptional scale and regularity of the repeat indicate a professional cartoon-based loom weaving.

title: Charcoal Ground Moroccan Tribal Banded Field Rug
lead: Woven in the Berber tradition of the Middle Atlas, this piece organizes its field into horizontal bands of chevron, lattice diamond, and dashed-line registers — each motif drawn from a vocabulary centuries older than the rug itself.

designFeatures: Sparse large-diamond row with interior striping
distinguishing: Pronounced tonal contrast between near-black ground and undyed natural wool pile motifs
Irregular hand-drawn quality to linework consistent with authentic Berber village weaving
Band sequencing follows traditional Azilal compositional structure

title: Moroccan Berber Lattice Diamond Pile Rug
lead: A hand-knotted Berber rug worked in undyed natural wool, its field organized by an allover trellis of elongated diamonds outlined in dark charcoal. The pile carries the characteristic texture of Atlas highland fleece.

distinguishing: Natural dye variation across the field suggests hand-spun highland wool of varying fleece batches.
The trellis lines are formed by knotted relief rather than color contrast alone, giving the pattern tactile depth.
Zigzag selvage border at the upper edge is consistent with traditional Beni Ourain-influenced urban workshop production.

title: Moroccan Ivory Ground Diamond Lattice Runner
lead: A well-proportioned Moroccan runner worked in undyed ivory wool, its field organized by an allover diamond-within-diamond lattice drawn in charcoal grey. The slightly irregular hand of the line gives the geometry warmth without sacrificing clarity.

designFeatures: Open field with no formal border
distinguishing: Undyed, naturally sourced wool pile contributes to tonal depth across the ivory field.
The slightly trembling hand-drawn line quality is characteristic of Beni Ourain-influenced contemporary weaving.
Runner proportion makes it well-suited to corridor or hallway placement without visual interruption.

title: Antique Indian Agra Allover Medallion Field Carpet
lead: Woven in the Agra workshops at the turn of the last century, this carpet presents a repeating field of lobed floral medallions on a warm sage ground, framed by an ivory border of naturalistic botanical forms rendered with characteristic Agra clarity.

designFeatures: Secondary guard stripes with geometric reciprocal motifs
Interconnecting vine and palmette fill across field
distinguishing: Tonal restraint across field creates a silvered, aged patina consistent with early Agra production.
Border scale is generously proportioned relative to field, a hallmark of the Agra tradition.
Overall color harmony suggests deliberate use of abrash to animate the sage ground.

title: Round Indo-Persian Striped Allover Botanical Field Rug
lead: A composed allover ground of horizontal botanical bands in warm khaki, sage, and muted coral moves across this generous round format with the quiet rhythm of a manuscript border repeated field-wide.

designFeatures: Repeating botanical vine and rosette bands
Round cut with fringed edge finish
Tonal warm ground with polychrome accents
distinguishing: Unusual round format preserves full symmetry of the striped repeat across a circular field
Palette deliberately muted to evoke aged Persian court textiles
Dense band count suggests a fine-knotted wool construction suited to high-traffic placement

title: Tibetan Cloud-and-Foliage Field Carpet Lavender Ground
lead: A field dense with auspicious cloud scrolls — the classic Tibetan chi pattern — rendered in warm ivory against a muted lavender-grey ground, enclosed by a sinuous border of naturalistic acanthus-like foliage.

designFeatures: Relief pile carving defining motif edges
distinguishing: The cloud motifs follow the traditional Tibetan auspicious symbol convention, distributed without formal lattice structure for an organic, floating quality.
Border foliage drawn in a more Western botanical style, suggesting a workshop bridging Tibetan convention with Western interior market taste.
Palette restraint — no saturated hues — makes this piece adaptable across neutral contemporary and transitional interiors.

title: Tibetan Ceremonial Rug with Dragon and Cloud Motifs
lead: A commanding field of warm ivory ground carries scattered auspicious cloud formations, a lattice-panel fragment, honeycomb medallion band, and a blue-ground dragon cartouche — characteristic of Tibetan palatial weaving at the turn of the last century.

designFeatures: Blue-ground dragon cartouche in lower field
Diagonal lattice panel fragment upper left
Scattered auspicious botanical and flame motifs
Saffron fringe border on all sides
distinguishing: The isolated dragon panel rendered in blue-grey suggests a repaired or intentionally fragmented compositional reference to a larger palatial textile tradition.
The horizontal striations in the ground weave are consistent with traditional Tibetan weft-float technique used to create tonal depth.
Asymmetric layout and collage-like motif placement indicate a workshop piece drawing from multiple canonical design sources rather than a single pattern repeat.

title: Swedish Flat-Woven Panel Rug in Celadon and Grass Green
lead: A composed Swedish flatweave built around a large central panel of layered grass and bottle greens, framed by a cream ground with quiet plaid banding. The blue accent lines read as a structural grid rather than ornament.

designFeatures: Plaid border banding in cream and taupe
Blue rectilinear grid overlay on green field
distinguishing: Asymmetric composition suggests a design study or single production piece rather than a commercial repeat.
The interplay of woven plaid ground with painted or overdyed green panel is characteristic of mid-century Swedish workshop practice.

title: Scandinavian Flatweave Grid Repeat on Silver Ground
lead: A Swedish rölakan-tradition flatweave organized around a disciplined grid of interlocking frames. Charcoal rectangles, ivory connectors, and muted gold accent squares move across a cool silver-grey field in an allover repeat consistent with mid-century Swedish studio practice.

designFeatures: Striped textural fill within each panel
Cruciform connector motifs at grid intersections
Graduated charcoal-to-slate tonal variation across panels
distinguishing: The alternating depth of charcoal within each rectangle suggests deliberate tonal sequencing rather than dye variation, a mark of considered studio design.
Ivory connector brackets at each grid junction create a secondary geometric lattice readable independently of the primary frame pattern.
Palette and geometric discipline align closely with weavings produced in Swedish cooperative workshops active between approximately 1945 and 1970.

title: Geometric Brick-Pattern Gabbeh in Autumn Tones
lead: A tightly composed all-over lattice of staggered rectangles moves across the field in warm terracotta, burgundy, and sand, each block carrying subtle tonal variation that keeps the geometry from becoming mechanical.

designFeatures: abrash-like tonal shifting within color fields
warm autumnal palette restricted to four tones
horizontal rhythm offset by vertical stripe accents
distinguishing: The absence of a formal border gives the pattern a modernist, borderless quality suited to contemporary interiors.
Subtle pile variation creates a light-and-shadow effect across the surface, adding depth beyond the flat geometry.
Color gradation within individual blocks suggests hand-dyeing in small batches rather than uniform commercial dye lots.

title: Charcoal Ground Abstract Stripe Wool Rug
lead: A quiet, considered piece built on a deep charcoal ground with loosely drawn black horizontal striations evoking brushwork on stone. The restraint of the palette gives it uncommon versatility in modern interiors.

distinguishing: Stripe intervals vary subtly, avoiding mechanical repetition
Low dense pile reads as near-flat, lending a refined tactile quality
Coloration consistent across full field with no visible fading

title: Tone-on-Tone Abrash Field Contemporary Hand-Knotted Rug
lead: A quietly considered field rug woven in a single warm camel ground, its character drawn entirely from natural abrash movement across the wool pile. No border interrupts the open weave plane.

designFeatures: open field with no formal border
tonal variation through natural wool dye lots
distinguishing: The abrash is genuine and unforced, lending the field a lived-in warmth uncommon in production pieces.
Aspect ratio and handle suggest a traditional room-size format suited to minimal interiors.

title: Silver-Lavender Distressed Allover Contemporary Hand-Knotted Rug
lead: A quietly commanding field of erased traditional motifs rendered in tonal silver, lavender, and muted plum. The intentional abrash and worn-finish treatment give depth without artifice, suited to understated interiors.

designFeatures: Tonal border with dot-and-dash inner guard
distinguishing: Deliberate degrade technique dissolves traditional motifs into abstraction
Subtle teal highlights emerge in upper field, adding chromatic interest
Silk content contributes low-level sheen across the field without heaviness

title: Lavender Field Trellis Hand-Tufted Wool Rug
lead: A soft lavender ground carries an ivory lattice in gently curved crossings, producing the classic Moroccan-influenced trellis in a thoroughly modern colorway suited to nursery, study, or sitting room alike.

distinguishing: Pattern lines show slight texture relief suggesting hand-tufted loop-cut construction.
Colorway favors cool violet-blue rather than the warmer mauves typical of machine-made versions.
No visible border — pattern runs edge to edge in a contemporary frameless format.

title: Sage Field Ziegler-Style Floral Scatter Rug
lead: A muted sage field carries scrolling vines, oak-leaf palmettes, and tulip blossom heads in the Ziegler manner, framed by a terracotta cartouche border with repeating rosette guards. Palette is earthy and restrained.

designFeatures: Open sage ground without central medallion
distinguishing: Design closely follows late 19th-century Ziegler Mahal commercial conventions adapted for the Indian export market.
Absence of a central medallion gives the field an allover, textile-like rhythm well-suited to scatter placement.
Color saturation is deliberately muted, suggesting a vegetable or low-chrome dye approach.

title: Contemporary Tribal Striped Gabbeh in Earth Tones
lead: A composed tribal weaving in the Gabbeh tradition, this piece organizes horizontal banded registers across a field of warm charcoal, sand, and muted gold — each row distinguished by a subtle shift in tone rather than explicit ornament.

designFeatures: Textural weft-face detailing at band borders
Minimalist allover format without central medallion
distinguishing: The alternating warm and cool tonal bands suggest natural, undyed or minimally processed wool.
Subtle blue-grey notes near the center field add depth without disrupting the earthy palette.
Border is absent — the composition relies entirely on interior rhythm for structure.

title: Neutral Tone Flatweave in Natural Brown Ground
lead: A plainweave field in interlocked natural and dark brown yarns, the overall tone settling into a warm, earthy neutral. The tight basket construction reads quietly in any room, lending texture without competing with furnishings.

distinguishing: Natural fiber character suggests jute or sisal blend in the weft
Tonal variation across the field gives subtle depth without formal pattern

title: Striped Flatweave in Warm Grey and Taupe
lead: A disciplined horizontal-stripe flatweave in alternating warm taupe and natural ivory, woven with a subtly textured weft that catches light evenly across the field. Understated and versatile, suited to both casual and considered interiors.

designFeatures: Textured weft surface with subtle ribbing
Plain selvage edges with minimal fringe
distinguishing: Consistent stripe cadence with no variation in width suggests mechanically guided hand-weaving
Neutral palette offers broad decorative compatibility

title: Persian Tabriz Runner with Repeating Medallion Field
lead: A long Tabriz runner carrying a column of eight fish-medallions across a densely patterned burgundy-to-rose field. The ivory border is crisp and the knotting fine, consistent with mid-century northwest Persian workshop production.

designFeatures: Reciprocal ivory main border with rosette chain
Graduated field color from deep burgundy to rose
distinguishing: Unusual tonal shift along the length suggests deliberate shading or natural dye variation
Medallion count of eight is consistent with a purpose-made gallery or corridor runner
Border proportion is generous relative to field width, a hallmark of quality Tabriz runners

title: Sage Field Ziegler Mahal Allover Floral Carpet
lead: Woven in the Ziegler commercial tradition of Sultanabad, this carpet deploys a relaxed allover lattice of botanical sprays across a muted sage field, contained by a cream-ground border of repeating rosettes and scrolling vine.

designFeatures: Repeating rosette and cartouche main border
Botanical spray infill in field compartments
distinguishing: Characteristic Ziegler palette of desaturated sage and ivory favored for Western interiors circa 1880-1910
Field color retains even tone suggesting consistent wool dye lot and low traffic history
Fringe intact at both ends, consistent with original foundation

title: Ottoman Tulip and Pomegranate Lattice Carpet
lead: A disciplined all-over lattice of Ottoman tulips and stylized pomegranates alternates across an ivory field, each repeat anchored by paired birds and feathered lancet leaves in muted teal and sage.

distinguishing: The borderless format is characteristic of court-influenced Anatolian weaving traditions.
Bird motifs woven into the lattice junctions suggest a connection to 16th-century Ottoman textile prototypes.
Ivory ground with low pile density preserves strong chromatic contrast between field and motif.

title: Antique Persian Mahal Allover Floral Field Carpet
lead: A large-format Mahal carpet woven in the Sultanabad tradition, its madder-red field carrying an allover repeat of rosette medallions, boteh forms, and serrated leaves arranged without a central focal point — a village interpretation of the classical harshang lattice.

designFeatures: Allover harshang-derived repeat without central medallion
Multi-tiered navy main border with palmette and vine scroll
Geometric rosette medallions in varied colorways across field
Reciprocal ivory guard borders framing the main border
Tribal boteh and serrated leaf infill elements
distinguishing: Field color retains strong natural madder saturation consistent with pre-synthetic dye period.
Slight asymmetry in repeat spacing indicates village rather than workshop production.
Navy border dye shows characteristic abrash typical of period indigo use.

title: Sage Field Tabriz Allover Floral Carpet
lead: A classic Tabriz allover design rendered on a soft sage ground, its symmetrical palmette vines unwinding with the quiet confidence of a practiced hand. The ivory border carries a secondary floral scroll that frames without competing.

designFeatures: Bilateral floral symmetry without central medallion
Multi-guard ivory border with floral scroll
distinguishing: The absence of a central medallion in favor of an uninterrupted field pattern suggests influence from the Safavid vase-carpet tradition adapted for the export trade.
Fringe integrity at both ends indicates minimal mechanical intervention over the rug's life.
The sage-green ground is characteristic of Tabriz workshops sourcing regional plant-based dyes blended with chrome mordants in the early twentieth century.
